column1: C[ohgrande: when you play a handicap game as white, what's the main difference?
Phelan: mostly that you need to complicate things
Phelan: some people think that white in handicap games needs to overplay, play unreasonable moves
Phelan: but it's mostly a matter of picking up the slack that Black gives
C[Phelan: some people think that white in handicap games needs to overplay, play unreasonable moves
Phelan: but it's mostly a matter of picking up the slack that Black gives
C[ohgrande: If you're feeling adventurous, rgatti and myself would apreciate your analysis and feedback of our recent game
C[Phelan: I've reviewed it, not sure if you've seen
C[ohgrande: thanks, I just saw it and read through it
ohgrande: some of it we saw and some of it was new
C[ohgrande: yes, it was very cool
ohgrande: between your feedback, playing and seeing what happens, and reading some introductory books, I feel like I have a good starting basis for improvment
ohgrande: although I think I just made an oversight just now with that last move
ohgrande: but, we'll see how it plays out

Phelan: and I'm glad that you're liking it :)

C[Phelan: there's a problem with J4.

C[rgatti: Yeah.  Last game, I think we got distracted in that complex center shape fairly early on.
rgatti: In this one, we've had smaller battles and more discrete shapes
C[rgatti: ...this is probably a mistake... but I want to try it anyway.

C[ohgrande: also, there's some learning to attack and defend in these spots that's valuable
rgatti: Yeah, that's why I wanted to try it out
rgatti: I know your stones at O17/O18 will complicate this
rgatti: (and your stone at R13 might have, had you decided to turn me in that direction, but less os)
C[rgatti: Your N14 move was great, especially since that was where I was going to play next... if you didn't
C[rgatti: I was hoping you wouldn't play T18...
C[rgatti: Yeah, I know... but there was hope... :-)

ohgrande: since you'd have to play R18 and R19 to get eyespace and that leaves you with the ""bulky 5"" eyeshape

rgatti: I think that was the move I needed to make to make that work...
C[rgatti: but I'll keep exploring this game :-)

ohgrande: I think you may be able to connect up underneath

rgatti: That's my hope... but I think if I had played P13 and you didn't respond with N16, I would have killed your O18 group.  There were a lot of scenarios that gave that result, but I overlooked P13 in my longterm analysis...
C[ohgrande: N15 was important so that you couldn't connect up
C[ohgrande: It looked like you were going for a capturing race

rgatti: I had a variety of things I was going after, depending on where you moved.  And I needed 3 liberties instead of 2 to capture your upper group (as you can see, if you didn't capture my stones when you did, I'd have captured yours on the next turn)
rgatti: And if you block differently, I might have captured your upper right stones instead
rgatti: So, it was certainly a couple of errors on my part
rgatti: But, overall, I'm actually surprised that it was as close as it was given how strong you appeared when I first started the invasion
C[ohgrande: my stones got clumpy and awkward
ohgrande: but you're right, a few different choices and you probably could have picked it off
C[ohgrande: I'm learning that identifying that 1 extra liberty is important when you need to decide to play or abort
ohgrande: I was playing against adiamant and I thought I was secure
ohgrande: and then, he just blew through my defense and it was ugly!
C[ohgrande: your last move was nice
rgatti: I expected it to be the move I'd need to make, regardless of where you played, so when you forced me into it, I was surprised and pleased
C[rgatti: I think you'd have done considerable damage to me if you had played R9,  At least, I couldn't think of a way to prevent it, but I didn't see it until after I had played

C[rgatti: This game has been fairly close.  I thought you had the edge after my invasion of your upper corner resulted in significant loss of pieces and position (though, somehow managed to create an awkward, but alive, position)
rgatti: I'm curious how close it turns out.
rgatti: There have been a few key moves that have significantly swung things one way or another, but overall, it has felt close and well-played

C[rgatti: NIce move!  I was kind of hoping I could bait you into overlooking that by letting you fill in the M3 hole so my M2 response didn't look like an attack.  But, clearly, you appeared to be as aware of that vulnerability as I was.
C[ohgrande: I'm trying to be more aware of when I have a shortage of liberties
ohgrande: to be honest, I nearly overlooked it!

C[rgatti: In retrospect, I wish I had played D5 instead of A9.  I'm not sure I'd have been able to do any real damage with D5, but there are certainly some interesting possibilities in the bottom left.
C[ohgrande: I think a9 was a bigger move
ohgrande: when you pinned that group in the middle of the board, that was the difference I think
ohgrande: also, those corner invasion sequences were interesting to play
rgatti: Yeah, pinning the group in the middle was a very large change.  Without that, this would be a very close game that could have gone either way

C[rgatti: So... what do you think of this game, so far?
ohgrande: opening strategy is a bit of a mystery
ohgrande: I've read the basics but will probably require many opportunities to practice!
C[rgatti: Yeah, I still don't have much of a clue on opening strategy
rgatti: Even with a massive handicap (stones in every corner), Phel still manages to rip apart my position with his early opening moves...
rgatti: I like what you did in the upper left.  I'm not sure if/how I can invade that position, and you managed that with only 2 stones.
ohgrande: playing on the 4-4 point allows for invasion
ohgrande: like in the upper-right, lower-right, lower-left
ohgrande: it also takes 3 stones to secure the corner if you play there
ohgrande: so, you're not secure in the lower-right yet
ohgrande: a stone at r5 will secure that corner though
ohgrande: and, like you saw in the lower-left, you needed a 3rd stone to secure that corner
ohgrande: although, I think e3 is slightly better in that position rather than d2
ohgrande: if you hadn't played there, I likely would have
ohgrande: what the 4-4 opening does do well is provide influence towards the center
ohgrande: so I have a fairly strong position up top towards the center
ohgrande: which is the tradeoff for you taking that corner
ohgrande: Now, the 3-4 point (c16) that I opened with is not as influential towards the center
ohgrande: but, it has the advantage that you can secure the corner in 2 moves with a knights-move (e17) from it
ohgrande: and that, is my opening theory information!
ohgrande: well, aside from some general points about the center star points, etc.
C[ohgrande: I like experimenting with the different points
ohgrande: a 3-3 opening would allow you to secure the corner with 1 stone
ohgrande: but, it seems pretty limited in it's future potential
ohgrande: I like to read and the go books are kind of interesting
ohgrande: but, it's more interesting when I can connect what I read to what I did when I played
ohgrande: like the ko-fight I had with sai
ohgrande: it actually started to make sense about how you can use ko and what ko threats are
ohgrande: this game is so deep
C[ohgrande: isn't it neat how each game is so va see tly different from each other?
ohgrande: ha ha, that's supposed to be vastly
C[rgatti: va see tly = vastly... I see it :-)
rgatti: Yes, the development of each game is a very unique experience each time
rgatti: Even the way the game develops in different stages is surprising.  What looks like it is going a certain way early often takes on very different characteristics later
rgatti: And, often, the final direction is highly dependent on minor placement variations of early stones that felt similar or minimally significant.
C[rgatti: For a while, I thought you were going to capture that group on the Q and 10 lines.  That was a very crafty attack :-)

ohgrande: pushing down though also put pressure on my side group so that suddenly became more important
C[rgatti: I think it helped a lot that I recognized the threat early.  That allowed me to make some stone placements I wouldn't have normally made just to try to gain connectivity.
rgatti: Still... I wasn't certain it would work.

rgatti: True.  Though, I think you had considerable strength over there by Move 17.  I was focusing elsewhere (especially in the upper right) and you were gaining more global strength
ohgrande: the global strength v local strength tradeoff is very subtle
rgatti: Yes, I'm certainly learning that.  And quite a bit even since the start of this game
ohgrande: I can really see why people like this game
ohgrande: well, it's lunch time over here so I'm gonna step away.
rgatti: Yes.  Though, I think it helps to like your opponent.  And for the games to move at a reasonable pace.
C[rgatti: I think this game is nearing an end, but it's been fun :-)
rgatti: And I look forward to future games with you as well :-)

rgatti: Thanks for the game!  That was a great experience!
